one_line_takeaway: Distilling from an empirically (adversarially) robust but formally unverifiable teacher into a CC-IBP certified-training student, via a bound-aware feature-space distillation loss, improves both standard and certified L-infinity accuracy over pure CC-IBP and sets a new ReLU-network state of the art on CIFAR-10, TinyImageNet, and downscaled ImageNet.
key_finding: CC-Dist improves both standard and certified accuracy over pure CC-IBP on every benchmark tested (Table 1). CIFAR-10 eps=2/255: CC-Dist reaches 81.55% standard / 64.60% certified accuracy vs. 79.51%/63.50% for the authors' own CC-IBP re-run and 80.09%/63.78% for the De Palma et al. (2024b) literature CC-IBP result. CIFAR-10 eps=8/255: CC-Dist reaches 55.13%/35.52% vs. 54.46%/35.42% (own CC-IBP) and 53.71%/35.27% (literature CC-IBP); on this specific setting SortNet (a 1-Lipschitz architecture, not ReLU) still attains a higher certified accuracy (40.39%) than any ReLU-based method. TinyImageNet eps=1/255: CC-Dist reaches 43.78%/27.88% vs. 41.28%/26.53% (own CC-IBP) and 38.71%/26.18% (literature EXP-IBP), a 'particularly remarkable' margin the authors attribute partly to using a smaller alpha than in the original CC-IBP paper. ImageNet64 eps=1/255: CC-Dist reaches 28.17%/13.96% vs. 25.94%/13.69% (own CC-IBP) and 22.73%/13.30% (literature EXP-IBP). CC-Dist thus sets a new state of the art for ReLU-network certified training on all benchmarks except CIFAR-10 eps=8/255 (where the 1-Lipschitz SortNet wins on certified accuracy only). Table 2 (teacher-student comparison, first 500 images) shows teachers have far higher standard/AutoAttack accuracy (e.g., CIFAR-10 eps=2/255: teacher 88.23% standard / 73.26% AA) but near-zero certified accuracy (1.2% and 0.0% on the two CIFAR-10 epsilons, 0.0% on ImageNet64), while the distilled students substantially surpass their own teachers in certified accuracy (e.g., 64.4% vs. 1.2% on CIFAR-10 eps=2/255) despite the teacher being empirically far stronger -- demonstrating that empirically-robust but unverifiable teachers can still transfer useful representations to a certifiably-robust student. A beta sensitivity study (Fig. 1, CIFAR-10) shows standard and empirical accuracy increase monotonically with beta, while certified accuracy (both branch-and-bound and incomplete-verifier-based) rises then falls, peaking at an intermediate beta, with the chosen 5/w value near this peak; the benefit is smaller for eps=8/255 than eps=2/255, attributed to greater teacher-student dissimilarity at the larger perturbation radius.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CC-Dist, a knowledge-distillation scheme for certified (formally verified) adversarial robustness training. It couples an existing 'expressive' certified-training loss (CC-IBP, from De Palma et al. 2024b) with a novel feature-space distillation loss that transfers knowledge from an empirically-robust (pure adversarially-trained) teacher network to a certifiably-robust student of the same architecture. The distillation term is defined as a convex combination (parameterized by the same alpha as CC-IBP) between IBP (Interval Bound Propagation) upper/lower bounds on student features and adversarial student features, matched against the teacher's clean features, with the combination weight beta fixed to 5/w (w = feature dimensionality) across all experiments. The authors prove (Propositions 3.1, 3.2, Lemma 3.3) that this distillation loss upper-bounds a worst-case feature-distillation objective and can be tightly coupled with CC-IBP via an affine relationship between the convex-combination features and the CC-IBP logit-difference bounds, when the student's classification head is affine.
threat_model: The setting is deterministic, complete-verifier certified robustness to L-infinity-norm adversarial perturbations on image classifiers (a feed-forward ReLU network f_theta must correctly classify every input within an L-infinity ball of radius epsilon around each training/test point). Robustness certificates are obtained via neural-network verification (branch-and-bound complete verifiers such as OVAL/alpha-beta-CROWN, and incomplete verifiers IBP/CROWN). The paper does not consider a novel attacker; it inherits the standard PGD/AutoAttack empirical-robustness threat model for the adversarially-trained teacher and the standard verification threat model (worst-case perturbation within Cepsilon(x)) for certification of the student.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/caveats: (1) On CIFAR-10 eps=8/255, ReLU-network CC-Dist still trails the specialized 1-Lipschitz SortNet architecture in certified accuracy; the authors note SortNet can be improved further with additional generated data (out of scope here) and defer investigating distillation for 1-Lipschitz networks to future work. (2) The teacher-student certified-accuracy comparison (Table 2) is computed on only the first 500 test images per benchmark because branch-and-bound verification on adversarially-trained teacher networks is too computationally expensive to run on the full test set -- an explicit methodological restriction the authors flag themselves. (3) Distillation benefit is smaller at larger perturbation radius (eps=8/255) where teacher and student representations diverge more. (4) The work only considers teachers with the same architecture as the student and explicitly flags this as a limitation, hoping future work explores teachers with larger effective capacity. (5) The approach is restricted to L-infinity perturbations, deterministic certificates, and ReLU feed-forward architectures (though appendices report some extension to other activations/residual architectures and other certified-training losses).

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 eps=2/255: CC-Dist 81.55% standard / 64.60% certified vs. own CC-IBP 79.51%/63.50% vs. literature CC-IBP (De Palma et al. 2024b) 80.09%/63.78%

CIFAR-10 eps=8/255: CC-Dist 55.13%/35.52% vs. own CC-IBP 54.46%/35.42% vs. literature CC-IBP 53.71%/35.27%; SortNet attains highest certified accuracy on this setup at 40.39% (54.84% standard) using a 1-Lipschitz architecture
TinyImageNet eps=1/255: CC-Dist 43.78%/27.88% vs. own CC-IBP 41.28%/26.53% vs. literature EXP-IBP 38.71%/26.18%
ImageNet64 eps=1/255: CC-Dist 28.17%/13.96% vs. own CC-IBP 25.94%/13.69% vs. literature EXP-IBP 22.73%/13.30%

Teacher vs CC-Dist student (Table 2, first 500 test images): CIFAR-10 eps=2/255 teacher 88.23% standard / 73.26% AutoAttack / 1.2% certified vs student 81.55%/70.71%/64.4%; CIFAR-10 eps=8/255 teacher 78.16%/41.62%/0.0% vs student 55.13%/36.80%/35.8%; TinyImageNet teacher 47.18%/34.17%/17.4% vs student 43.78%/32.30%/32.6%; ImageNet64 teacher 40.30%/25.34%/0.0% vs student 28.17%/17.87%/5.6%
Beta fixed at 5/w (w = feature dimensionality) across all main experiments
baselines_compared: SABR (Mao et al. 2024a / De Palma et al. 2024b)
IBP-R (Mao et al. 2024a / De Palma et al. 2022)
IBP (Mao et al. 2024a / Shi et al. 2021 / Gowal et al. 2019)
CROWN-IBP (Mao et al. 2025 / Xu et al. 2020)
